metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = continuum / exponential decay / golden rule rate; CRIMSON = discrete oscillation / reversible Rabi bounce
metadata.note: No sinc-squared-to-delta derivation, no density-of-states counting algebra, no Wigner-Weisskopf resummation.

beats.narration_text: Drive a two-level quantum system at resonance. One final state: population bounces back and forth forever. Give it two final states. Three. A hundred.
The oscillation stops being an oscillation. It becomes a smooth, one-way exponential decay — the same decay that sets the lifetime of every excited atom.
Why does adding more final states kill the oscillation and produce a constant decay rate?

props.headline: Why does adding more final states kill the oscillation and produce…

card.headline:
One final state: oscillation.
Many final states: decay. Why?
card.dek: From Rabi oscillation to Fermi's golden rule

beats.narration_text: With one final state, the transition amplitude builds coherently — the system knows exactly where to send population, and the exact same path brings it back.
Each additional final state contributes its own oscillating amplitude. They all start in phase but quickly diverge in frequency. Most of the time, they cancel.
Only at the very beginning do all the amplitudes point the same direction. After that one brief window, they dephase and cancel — all except the initial linear growth.

Summed across a continuum, the total probability grows linearly with time — a constant rate. That rate is Fermi's golden rule: W equals two pi over h-bar times the matrix element squared times the density of states.
The exponential lifetime follows immediately: the atom has no memory of when it entered the excited state, so it decays at a constant rate — tau equals one over W.

More available final states means a faster decay — the atom has more places to go. This is why radiative lifetimes depend on the photon density of states and why the Purcell effect can speed or slow an atom.

Illustrative: hydrogen 2p to 1s. Matrix element squared equals 0.55 a-naught squared times e squared. Photon density of states at 10.2 eV. Golden rule gives W equals 6 times ten to the eighth per second — lifetime 1.6 nanoseconds, matching experiment.
One oscillation becomes one-way decay when the final states are too many to bounce from. The rate is constant. The atom has no memory. That is Fermi's golden rule.

props.headline: One oscillation becomes one-way decay when the final states are too…

card.headline: W = (2π/ℏ) |V_fi|² ρ(E)
card.dek: Dephasing across a continuum converts reversible Rabi oscillation into one-way exponential decay.
